questions.question: Define a singly linked list and explain its basic components.
keyPoints: Linear data structure with nodes
Each node has data field and next pointer field
Nodes linked via next pointers from head to tail
Last node's next points to NULL

questions.question: Write an algorithm to insert a new node at the beginning of a singly linked list.
keyPoints: Create a new node using malloc
Assign data to the node's data field
Set new node's next to point to current head
Update head pointer to point to new node
Time complexity is O(1)

questions.question: Explain the process of deleting the first node from a singly linked list.
keyPoints: Store address of first node in temporary pointer
Move head pointer to point to second node
Free the memory of the original first node
Handle edge case when list has only one node
Time complexity is O(1)

questions.question: What are the advantages of linked lists over arrays?
keyPoints: Dynamic size - grows/shrinks at runtime
No memory wastage - no pre-allocation
Easy insertion and deletion without shifting elements
Flexible memory allocation

questions.question: What is traversal in a singly linked list? Write the algorithm for traversing and printing all elements.
keyPoints: Start from head node
Visit each node and process data
Move to next node using the next pointer
Repeat until next pointer is NULL
Time complexity is O(n)

questions.question: Explain the difference between malloc() and sizeof() in the context of creating nodes in C.
keyPoints: sizeof() returns size of data type in bytes
malloc() dynamically allocates requested bytes of memory
sizeof() does not allocate memory - just calculates size
malloc() returns pointer to allocated memory or NULL on failure
Both used together: malloc(sizeof(struct node))

questions.question: What are the disadvantages of singly linked lists compared to arrays?
keyPoints: No random access - must traverse sequentially
Extra memory overhead for storing pointers
Cannot traverse backwards easily
No direct access to previous node
Potential memory fragmentation

questions.question: Write a complete C program to create a singly linked list with 3 nodes, insert a node at the beginning, delete a node from the end, and display the final list.
keyPoints: Define struct node with data and next pointer
Function to create new node with malloc
Function to insert at beginning with O(1) complexity
Function to delete from end requiring traversal
Function to display list by traversal
Proper memory management with free()
Handle all edge cases properly

questions.question: Explain the algorithm to insert a new node after a given node in a singly linked list. Also write the algorithm to delete a node after a given node.
keyPoints: For insertion: check if given node is not NULL
Set new node's next to given node's next
Update given node's next to point to new node
For deletion: store node to delete in temporary pointer
Bypass the node by linking previous to next
Free the deleted node's memory
Handle NULL pointer cases
Time complexity O(1) for both operations

questions.question: Compare arrays and singly linked lists with respect to: (i) memory allocation (ii) access time (iii) insertion/deletion operations (iv) cache locality.
keyPoints: Memory: arrays contiguous, linked lists scattered
Access: arrays O(1), linked lists O(n)
Insertion/Deletion: arrays O(n) shifting, linked lists O(1) at ends
Cache: arrays have better locality due to contiguity
Space: arrays fixed, linked lists dynamic with pointer overhead
Use cases: arrays for indexed access, linked lists for frequent insertions/deletions

questions.question: Write an algorithm to search for an element in a singly linked list. Also explain how you would modify it to find the position of the middle element.
keyPoints: Search: traverse from head, compare each node's data
Return position if found, return NOT FOUND otherwise
Time complexity O(n)
For middle element: use two pointers (slow and fast)
Move slow one step, fast two steps
When fast reaches end, slow is at middle
This is the turtle and hare technique
Time complexity still O(n) with single pass
